title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several items including a resolution supporting state transportation funding, awarded a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, and directed staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines up to $100 per day. The committee also approved construction agreements for multiple road projects and acknowledged utility reports.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permit
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC annual report
Water and sewer connection agreement for Radkey Avenue
Construction service proposals for Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel Avenue projects

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project. The project will likely move from 2029 to 2028. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating, and manhole repair approaches for Schofield Avenue with various contractor methods being tested.

Staff reported on road limits removal, mailbox repairs, sign repairs, and spring sweeping operations. Dewatering was conducted on Weston Avenue tributary for about a week due to flooding. Committee discussed the ongoing flooding issues in the Zenzer/Shorey Avenue area and potential permanent pump solutions. Jones Street had culvert failures requiring installation of a third 18-inch culvert. Equipment maintenance and changeover from winter to summer operations was detailed.

Josh reported on water service repairs, well maintenance, and sanitary sewer issues. Significant clear water infiltration was identified - in April, 37 million gallons were put into the distribution system but 53-54 million gallons were treated, indicating roughly 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. Discussion focused on residents illegally connecting sump pumps to sanitary sewers. The committee noted increased costs of $30,000-$42,000 in additional treatment expenses due to the extra flow.

Michael presented the annual Public Service Commission report showing net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, consistent with prior year. Water loss was 6%. Utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. The utility serves 6,100 customers total with 5,250 in Weston and 830 in Rothschild. Approved unanimously to acknowledge.

The committee reviewed a League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution supporting long-term transportation funding solutions. Michael noted the village has decreased public works budgets while still needing recurring annual funding for street maintenance and snow plowing that grant funding doesn't cover. Approved unanimously to recommend to Village Board.

Michael explained the village's Ryan Street site where recycled concrete and asphalt is crushed into base course. King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23 per ton for an estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. Michael calculated the village saves approximately $11 per cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, resulting in about $130,000 savings on three projects this year. Awarded to King Gravel unanimously.

Committee discussed enforcement of ordinance violations for clear water discharge into sanitary sewers. Current code allows fines between $100-$1,000 per day. Committee members debated whether violations are unknowing versus willful. Committee directed staff to create enforcement language with fines not to exceed $100 per day, with the approach being to notify violators first and escalate fines for repeat offenders. Staff will also include notices in monthly bills and newsletters. Approved unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael presented an agreement allowing a duplex builder at 2230 Radkey Avenue to temporarily connect to water and sewer mains via long lateral service until Radkey Avenue reconstruction. Committee added language requiring the reconstruction and assessment to occur no later than the 2029 construction season. Approved unanimously to recommend to Village Board.

The committee reviewed an intergovernmental agreement with Rothschild for Military Road construction from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Weston's share for water main relocation and sanitary sewer is estimated at $94,860. Steam Construction is the contractor selected by Rothschild. Water main must be relocated due to storm sewer conflicts. Approved unanimously to recommend to Village Board.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For the Alderson/Jelnik roundabout project, MSA proposed $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910; MSA was recommended. For the Bladel Avenue project, MSA proposed $70,400 with a summer intern versus Clark Dietz at $73,730 with full-time staff; Clark Dietz was recommended due to staff experience for the additional $3,000. Both recommendations approved unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23 per ton (estimated $126,900)
Staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines not to exceed $100 per day
Recommend Village Board adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Recommend Village Board authorize Radkey Avenue water/sewer connection agreement with assessment no later than 2029
Recommend Village Board approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild (estimated $94,860)
Approve MSA for Alderson/Jelnik roundabout construction services ($37,315)
Approve Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue construction services ($73,730)
Special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue scheduled for Thursday, May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Norcon to begin Schofield Avenue concrete repairs starting Wednesday/Thursday this week
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration treatment costs for next meeting
Staff to look at adding street lighting to Schofield Avenue project from Birch Street to Camp Phillips Road

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its first meeting after reorganization, electing officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing hot air balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially boosting attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Election of officers (Chair, Vice Chair, Secretary)
Work product transmittals, educational presentations and reports
New business - Grant request for Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated for Chair and approved unanimously. Cushing was nominated for Vice Chair by motion, seconded by Jackson, and approved unanimously. Renee was nominated and approved unanimously as Secretary.
Minutes from April 21st were approved with a motion and second by Renee. Passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill of 3308 Green Pastures Lane presented on behalf of the Taste and Glow Balloonfest, reporting estimated attendance of 11,000-12,000 last year. He announced that a competing Marshfield balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making theirs the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commission members questioned hotel room usage, noting the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year. The commission requested better tracking of hotel room data for future applications. A motion was made to approve $5,000 (same as last year), seconded by Jackson, and passed unanimously.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates, noting winner Anastasia had three bachelor's degrees. This was the first time Marathon County hosted the event since the 1970s. The candidates visited local attractions including Mullen's Cheese and expressed appreciation for the Marathon County community.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest for their annual event
Taste and Glow organizers to provide hotel room usage data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved a site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection districts. The commission also scheduled the first steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan update for June 1st.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Salt storage shed inspection program - Wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update

discussions.item: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility site plan
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. Jennifer explained the building meets all B2 zoning requirements and was rotated to address a gas pipeline easement running through the property. Chief Jeremy explained the building was designed for future growth, with current capacity for 40 officers but room for 50-60. Discussion covered the alternate storage shed placement, with commissioners suggesting it be positioned further north to allow future building expansion. The motion to approve was made and passed unanimously with conditions 2-5 as listed by staff.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program - Wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Jennifer presented the proposed inspection program requiring two annual inspections per facility - one warm weather inspection focused on structural integrity and drainage, and one winter operational inspection. The program includes a standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT requirements. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process despite being closest to the village well. Commissioners agreed staff should reach out to that facility. The motion to approve passed unanimously, with an amendment to require reporting back to both the Plan Commission and Public Works Committee.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
discussions.body: Motion to approve was made, seconded, and passed unanimously without discussion.
Motion to approve was made, seconded, and passed unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly project update
discussions.body: Jennifer announced the first steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan update is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m., with joint participation from the Plan Commission and Village Board. She committed to sending calendar invites and emails to commissioners.

discussions.item: Future agenda items and residential TIF discussion
discussions.body: Electric vehicle charging stations were noted for a future fall agenda. A commissioner asked about potential residential TIF districts for housing development. Staff indicated Ehlers has prepared a presentation on residential TIF qualifications that could be shared with the commission, possibly as part of the comprehensive plan steering committee discussions.

publicComment: Jim Penson of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online connection. He urged the commission to exercise common sense on landscaping requirements for the new police department to avoid excessive taxpayer costs similar to the Green Heck Turner building project. He also requested that the salt shed inspection program include annual reporting on site visits and findings, noting that some zoning complaints are years old.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive approved with staff conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with twice-annual inspections
Staff to report inspection findings to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee annually
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg that lacks conditional use approval
First comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send calendar invites and Ehlers residential TIF presentation to commissioners
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be addressed in fall 2026

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an orientation meeting for new board members, featuring educational presentations from department heads on the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, and Aging Disability Resource Center. The committee approved April 1st meeting minutes unanimously and received detailed briefings on department missions, budgets, and current initiatives including homelessness response, veteran suicide prevention, and challenges with nutrition program funding.

agenda.item: Call to order and remote participation confirmation
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute discussion
Review of standing committee ordinance, missions, duties and responsibilities

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion passed unanimously with all members voting in favor.

Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's jurisdiction under section 2.04 sub5, covering departments including Veteran Services, Social Services, and Health Department, plus affiliated entities North Central Health Care and Aging Disability Resource Center. He outlined relationships with various boards including the Social Services Board, Veteran Service Commission, Transportation Coordinating Committee, and Board of Health. Chair Boots noted the strategic plan development will be a focus through December, with meetings aligned to strategic objectives.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission of preventing disease, promoting health, and protecting from environmental hazards. The department has 37.6 FTE across four teams: environmental health, nursing, community health improvement, and administration. Key initiatives include public health vending machines with free tick kits and harm reduction supplies, the Nurse Family Partnership program with first graduates (two-year-olds), and a new homeless system response position. The department manages over 1,200 licenses and has measles response plans in place. Supervisor Kremes asked about pandemic preparedness, and Suginaka confirmed extensive training, response plans, and staffing strategies developed from COVID experience.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa, noting the department has nearly 140 FTE with a 2026 budget over $22 million, of which approximately $7 million is tax levy. He highlighted the Family Keys housing stability pilot program, developed with state funding and county board input, which addresses root causes of housing instability. The department covers child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. Leonard indicated upcoming requests for restructuring within units due to increased need.

Veteran Service Officer Erin Galindo reported the department grew from 2.75 to 3.0 FTE and saw customer interactions increase from approximately 5,900 to 8,149 in 2025. Veterans in Marathon County receive $44 million annually in benefits, with $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Key initiatives include suicide prevention efforts (7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans), housing stability partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues, and PACT Act claims processing. The department is working with hospice facilities and the medical examiner's office to improve death certificate documentation for surviving spouse benefits. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional collaboration; Galindo confirmed good working relationships with neighboring counties while noting state statute requires each county to maintain its own VSO.

Executive Director Mike Ray presented on the four-county regional ADRC serving Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood counties. The organization has about 60 FTE and 385-400 volunteers, with 11% county levy funding. Major challenges include nutrition program costs rising 40-45% over four years while federal Older Americans Act funding remains flat at 2024 levels. The organization hadn't requested a levy increase from 2011 until 2024. Services include Meals on Wheels, Medicare/Medicaid benefit specialists, dementia care specialists, and falls prevention programs. Wisconsin is number one nationally in falls and falls-related injuries. Ray noted staff have had to reduce meals served, delivery days, and staffing to address budget constraints.

actionItems: April 1st committee meeting minutes approved
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation before the committee
Mike Ray to present ADRC annual report with 2025 data to full board
Next meeting corrected to Wednesday, June 3rd at 3 PM (not May 29th as initially stated)
Committee members encouraged to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with agenda item requests
Strategic plan development to proceed with goal of completion by December

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and held extensive discussion about parking and logistics challenges at the relocated farmers market at Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and made committee appointments.

agenda.item: Board of Review adjournment to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees called to order, Pledge of Allegiance, Roll Call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions
Consent agenda - vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances - mobile food vendor, standing committees, nuisances, e-bikes
Unfinished business - remote meeting attendance policy
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.body: Staff presented proposed fee increases from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units, consistent with the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke in public comment expressing concern that there is no real data justifying the specific numbers. Trustee Barb supported the fees as justified given population growth. Ordinance 26-010 was approved unanimously and Resolution 2026-015 was approved unanimously.

Chief Finkey explained the ordinance creates a regional agreement with Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, Clover, Riverside, and Safer fire departments for mutual recognition of food truck fire inspections. A truck inspected by any participating department receives a sticker recognized by all, eliminating redundant inspections. Ordinance 26-008 was approved unanimously.

The board debated amendments to the policy governing virtual attendance. Trustee Beck raised concerns that the draft language requiring health, safety, or welfare justifications did not match what was discussed at the previous meeting. After discussion, Trustee Beck moved to amend the policy to strike sentences requiring specific justifications, making it more broad. The amendment passed with two no votes from Trustees Hermeling and Lobsral. The original motion as amended also passed with the same two no votes.

The president appointed Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermeling, second by Lobsral, approved unanimously. The board agreed to include committee applicant resumes in future board packets.

The board held extensive discussion-only on the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park after losing the Camp Phillips/Schofield Avenue location to development. Trustee Beck expressed safety concerns about cars parking on grass along Alderson. Renee explained the market grew from 25 stalls at the old location to 53 spots at Kennedy Park, and vendors appreciate having vehicles with them. Multiple trustees acknowledged the difficulty finding an ideal location, with Tamach Miller Park rejected due to poor visibility. Renee confirmed the market is not running at a loss due to grants and vendor fees. No action was taken.

Public Works Director Michael reported on the speed study for Weston Avenue. The DOT engineering study indicated 35 mph but the 85th percentile speed was 45 mph. He noted the road is currently under construction so additional data collection is delayed. The matter will return to a future board meeting.

The board approved a secondhand article dealer and secondhand jewelry dealer license for Schofield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street, with an amendment requiring satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit. Chief explained these licenses are required under state statute for businesses buying and selling used personal property, with a 30-day hold period to check for stolen merchandise. Motion by Hermeling, second by Clark, approved unanimously as amended.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 5102 Street spoke during public comment criticizing the board for hiring outside engineering consultants ($108,000 estimated) rather than filling the vacant engineering technician position. He also criticized the appointment process for committees, noting Lisa Beck was introduced as a Safer board member before official appointment. He expressed frustration that no detailed Kennedy Park information was included in the packet despite prior board requests. During the impact fee public hearing, Pitel again spoke expressing concern about lack of real data justifying the specific fee amounts.
actionItems: Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Ordinance 26-010 approved increasing park and recreation impact fees
Resolution 2026-015 approved amending village fee schedule
April 13 and April 21, 2026 meeting minutes approved
Ordinance 26-008 approved for mobile food vendor license regional agreement
Ordinance 26-009 approved amending standing committees section
Ordinance 26-011 approved amending nuisances chapter
Ordinance 26-012 approved amending bicycle regulations for e-bikes
Resolution 2026-014 approved supporting long-term transportation funding solution
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove specific justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Secondhand dealer license approved for Schofield Coin and Hobby LLC pending background check and occupancy
Compliance assurance plan approved
Consent agenda approved including vouchers and all license renewals
Weston Avenue speed limit issue to return to future board meeting after additional speed study data
Comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 PM
Committee applicant resumes to be included in future board packets

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board approved committee appointments for the new term, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the executive committee, and unanimously approved a resolution to reduce landfill tipping fees for storm debris following the recent tornado and flooding disaster. The board heard from affected Ringle residents requesting additional fee relief beyond the proposed reduction.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Election of at-large executive committee members
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 26-26: Modification to landfill tipping fees

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing diverse perspectives, developing new supervisors as leaders, and honoring supervisor preferences. Over 34% of the board is in its first or second term. Supervisor Cavelli moved and Supervisor Crims seconded approval of both county board chair and administrator appointments. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of at-large executive committee members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated for two at-large executive committee positions: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg spoke in support of Supervisor Leur, citing her advocacy for children and elders. Paper ballots were distributed and counted. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the executive committee.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Modification to landfill tipping fees
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented a proposal to reduce tipping fees for disaster-related debris following the recent tornado and flooding. The standard $65/ton rate would be reduced to $36.94/ton for certified storm debris, with the state waiving $13.10, Ringle waiving $2.33, and the county reducing its portion. The board suspended rules unanimously to consider the resolution. Supervisor Rosenberg requested public comment, and two Ringle residents spoke: Jessica Tolesie asked the county to waive fees entirely for six months like other entities, and Matt Haybeck requested relief for underinsured homeowners facing demolition costs. Multiple supervisors asked questions about eligibility, timeline, and costs. Administrator Leonard noted ongoing discussions with the Community Foundation about covering unreimbursed out-of-pocket costs. The resolution passed unanimously.

publicComment: Two Ringle residents were granted special dispensation to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Tolesie requested Marathon County waive fees entirely for six months, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle are waiving their portions, and that private businesses have donated $40,000+ in cleanup efforts. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of affected homeowners, requesting fee relief for underinsured residents whose insurance adjusters haven't yet arrived, noting approximately 44 homes were affected.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved for the new term
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to executive committee at-large positions
Resolution 24-26 approving out of home placement reserve increase approved (not unanimous)
Resolution 25-26 amending social services budget approved (not unanimous)
Resolution 26-26 reducing landfill tipping fees for storm debris to $36.94/ton through June 17th approved unanimously
Administrator to continue discussions with Community Foundation on reimbursing unreimbursed out-of-pocket disaster costs
Solid waste management board to develop clear policy on handling similar disaster situations within six months
Board may revisit tipping fee reduction timeline at May meeting if extension needed

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and forwarded an amended fee waiver resolution to the county board that will only cover septic reconnect fees for tornado-affected properties. The committee also received department overviews from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential) - Town of Green Valley
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (conservancy recreation to general agriculture) - Town of McMillan
Consideration of fee waivers for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Department overview: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department
Department overview: Conservation, Planning, and Zoning Department

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes with Supervisor Drebeck seconding. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential for a proposed land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered for, against, or as interested. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and town approval; Supervisor Kurt seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from conservancy recreation to general agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature, and Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable - the rezoning would make these conforming uses rather than non-conforming. No natural areas would be lost. Town of McMillan recommended approval April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefelt moved to approve; Supervisor Drew seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waivers for April 17, 2026 severe weather impacted properties
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented staff recommendations to waive county zoning and septic fees for properties damaged by the April 17th tornado in Ringle, Kronenwetter, Eastston, and Weston. Significant debate occurred with Supervisor Drew questioning fairness for future smaller disasters, Supervisor Leur asking about budget impacts, and Supervisor Drebeck expressing concerns about precedent. Eric Vogel asked if homeowners insurance would cover fees. Administrator Lance Leonhard emphasized this was triggered by the governor's emergency declaration. Supervisor Sefelt moved to forward the resolution; Supervisor Leur seconded. Supervisor Drebeck then amended the motion to limit fee waivers to only septic reconnect fees ($160), excluding all other zoning and pouts fees. Supervisor K seconded the amendment. The amendment carried. The amended motion then carried, though not unanimously.

David Hagenbuscher presented Solid Waste overview covering landfill operations, the new renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, leachate treatment challenges requiring $6 million in capital projects, and PFAS regulations requiring treatment by 2032. Tom Lovelin presented Parks, Recreation and Forestry overview noting the department serves both city and county, operates 17 county parks and 30,700 acres of county forest, and that Nine Mile generates $3.25 million for the local economy. Supervisor Sefelt announced Tom Lovelin is retiring June 1st after nearly 30 years of service.

actionItems: Rezoning petition for James Offer and David Tract approved - forwarded to County Board
Rezoning petition for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz approved - forwarded to County Board
Amended fee waiver resolution (limited to septic reconnect fees only) forwarded to County Board for consideration
CPZ staff to track costs and requests related to tornado fee waivers for future policy development
Joe Tucker selected as new County Forest Administrator to replace retiring Tom Lovelin

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center due to the chair's absence, approved installation of new kayak launch signs for the Plover River Water Trail, and received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising and the Yellow Banks disc golf course tree removal project. Public commenters voiced opposition to dogs at the pool and questioned Kennedy Park financial transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail discussion

discussions.body: Minutes from the April 27, 2026 meeting were approved unanimously with a motion and second. No discussion occurred.

Director Sean Oster provided a timeline document showing the project history from the comprehensive plan update through the May 9th ribbon cutting. Committee member Scott asked about village capital funds, with Oster confirming no village dollars are currently earmarked pending fundraising progress. The Friends of Kennedy Park are targeting $117,000 for upper field lights, currently at approximately $84-85,000 in the community foundation. A committee member defended the Friends group, noting they are volunteers who plan to announce fundraising totals at their June tournament and deserve 'a little bit of grace.'

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Dan presented photos showing fallen trees removed from the river and wood mat installation over gas pipelines to protect them during equipment access. The mats are approximately 8x8 timber pieces owned by the village, with extras potentially available for other trail projects. TC Energy (formerly ATC/ANR pipeline) conducted site visits during the work.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: Discussion was deferred to the June meeting because Katrina, who requested the agenda item, had an unforeseen issue preventing attendance. The chair noted he wanted her to have the opportunity to speak on the issue she raised.

Staff explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete and benches, noting the best tool (sandblaster pressure washer) cannot access the trail due to steep ditches and narrow bridges. Suggestions included anti-vandalism coatings and organizing volunteer workdays. Memorial benches weighing 2,000 lbs each could potentially be removed one at a time for off-site cleaning. No formal action was taken; staff will investigate options.

Dan Lepper from Marathon County Parks (2625 Northwestern Avenue) presented version 7 of signs for the Plover River Water Trail. Signs will be installed at launches 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, and possibly 8, with three in the Village of Weston. The Highway J launch will likely be closed all season due to bridge replacement. Signs include QR codes for river gauge data and donation links to Marathon County Parks Foundation. Motion to approve installation passed unanimously.

Staff requested committee interest in food allergy awareness signage for playgrounds, noting one in 13 people have food allergies. Signs would be provided free and encourage keeping food away from play equipment. Committee agreed to bring it back for formal discussion. The Mock Miller Park master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting with Rettler.

publicComment: Two speakers offered public comment. Andy (6504 [address unclear]) spoke against allowing dogs at the aquatic center, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' Jim Pensan (5002 Street, Weston), calling in under the name 'Vern M' due to login issues, criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation and questioned Kennedy Park financial reporting, calling the $8,700 figure 'too coincidental' and asking where grant money and glow event proceeds are tracked, stating 'I do not understand why there is such a lack of cooperation and hesitation from the friends group to actually share the finances.'
actionItems: Dog Day at aquatic center discussion deferred to June 22, 2026 meeting
Kayak launch signs approved for installation at Village of Weston launches
Staff to investigate graffiti removal options and potential anti-vandalism coatings for trail amenities
Food allergy awareness signage discussion to be added to future agenda
Mock Miller Park master plan kickoff meeting scheduled with Rettler
June 11th bike and pedestrian plan open house at training room, 5-7 p.m.

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged budget reports before receiving an extensive educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam on the clerk's office operations, elections, and licensing. Committee member Evan Harling announced his resignation due to relocating outside the village.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's office budget and operations
Staff remarks and farmers market update
Committee member remarks and resignation announcement

discussions.body: Motion to approve minutes from the previous Finance and HR meeting was made by Ernling and seconded by Daniels. Motion carried with no discussion.

The committee reviewed the April budget status report, TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports, and legal details report. Regarding TIF closure, Jessica explained the village is anticipating October/November for final TIF closure, will need CDA resolution to call the bonds due October 1st, and confirmed the village will be able to pay off the debt. Motion to acknowledge items 5, 6, and 7 was made by Daniels, seconded by Soaper. Motion carried.

Clerk Pam presented extensively on the clerk's office operations, staffing (currently 2 full-time and 1 part-time employee, down from 4 full-time plus 2 assistants previously), elections administration, licensing, tax collection, and pet licensing. She noted the office collected 84% of tax payments ($22 million) at the counter. The committee discussed the recent pet licensing fee increase from $10 to $20 for neutered animals, which was prompted by Marathon County raising fees to municipalities to help cover their $100,000 annual payment to the Humane Society. Committee members questioned the $175 per animal cost for non-dog strays, including eight mice picked up last year. Newsletter costs of approximately $30,000 annually for printing and postage were discussed, with suggestions to explore cost savings through email distribution or coordinating with other municipalities.

Staff member Renee reported the Kennedy Park farmers market has been going well after three Saturdays of operation. Tuesdays have been challenging due to cool and windy conditions. The market has expanded to include coffee vendors, new food trucks, and craft vendors, with produce vendors expected to increase mid to late June. Committee member Daniels raised concerns about parking on Alderson Street, with staff clarifying parking is now allowed on the west side only, while Newport has never had parking restrictions.
Evan Harling announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though remaining in the metro area. Committee members thanked him for his service.

actionItems: CDA to pass resolution this summer to call TIF bonds due October 1st
Staff to reach out to Ehlers regarding TIF bond closure process
Clerk's office to consider increasing chief inspector pay from $13 to $15 per hour for next election term
Committee seeking citizens to fill open committee spots including Harling's vacancy
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee approved amendments to the nuisance ordinance, recommended an e-bike ordinance with restrictions on emotos, and authorized demolition of an abandoned structure. The committee also discussed overnight winter parking regulations and a crosswalk request from Mount Olive Church, deferring both for further research.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Overnight parking regulations October 31st to May 1st
Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendment

discussions.item: Code enforcement, Fire/EMS, and Police department reports
discussions.body: Reports were acknowledged from all three departments. Roman noted 3020 Mountain View Avenue will come off the code enforcement roll after the property sale completed and new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt reported the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations. The department has hired its 40th officer. Two SROs work on zoning violations during summer for Weston, Rothschild, and Schofield. Motion by Chwanka, seconded by Olsen, passed unanimously to acknowledge reports.

Committee reviewed final copy of nuisance ordinance amendments previously discussed, including tree limb height requirements of 14 and 10 feet over streets and walkways. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Compie to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees. Motion passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion on regulating e-bikes and emotos. Chief Hunt advocated prohibiting emotos from roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Committee discussed whether electric scooters should be included and decided to leave enforcement discretion to officers. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend approval with the addition that emotos are prohibited from operating on roadways, sidewalks, trails, or any public property in the Village of Weston. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Overnight parking regulations October 31st to May 1st
discussions.body: Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction (4am-4pm) is far outside the norm compared to surrounding municipalities which typically have 4-5 hour windows overnight. Michael Wodalski from DPW indicated plowing rarely occurs before 4am and suggested signing specific problem areas instead. Chief Hunt preferred keeping overnight restrictions to give officers time to clear vehicles before plowing. Committee decided to defer to July, asking Wodalski to identify specific problem areas requiring exceptions.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest high school parking lot. Committee debated whether to install a basic crosswalk (approximately $2,000-3,000) or lighted system ($25,000-30,000). Chief Hunt reported no safety incidents at the location. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend board approval if Mount Olive agrees to cover installation costs. Motion passed unanimously. Michael Wodalski will contact Mount Olive.

discussions.item: Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendment
discussions.body: Fire Chief explained a regional fire inspection program with Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Safer. Any participating department can inspect food trucks and issue a sticker valid for all participating municipalities. One recent inspection failed due to improper LP piping and unsecured propane containers. Motion by Chwanka, seconded by Compie to approve Ordinance 26-8. Motion passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing two addresses), 3917 Ross Avenue (out of sequence with apartment), and 2211 Radkey Avenue (new duplex on private drive). Chief Hunt mentioned another issue at Weston Villa Apartments with eight separate addresses for one structure. Motion to recommend approval to board passed unanimously.

Structure has been abandoned since 2010-2014 and was previously used as a chicken coop. Fire department deemed it too unsafe for a training burn. Current owners prefer the village proceed with demolition; costs will be added to tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Compie to recommend board authorization for raze order. Motion passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding multiple agenda items. He criticized the new farmers market location at the park, predicting grass destruction by summer's end and questioning whether parking vehicles on grass violates village code. He noted ADA handicap spaces are signed on grass rather than the adjacent asphalt, which he argued violates ADA requirements for firm level surfaces. He questioned the new fire inspection requirement for mobile food vendors, asking where it originated. He also raised concerns about food truck regulations on public property and suggested some code enforcement cases have been pending too long without visible progress.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 nuisance amendments to Board of Trustees for approval
Recommend e-bike ordinance to Board of Trustees with emoto prohibition on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property
Michael Wodalski to identify specific areas requiring winter parking exceptions for July meeting
Michael Wodalski to contact Mount Olive Church regarding cost-sharing for crosswalk installation
Approve Ordinance 26-8 for regional mobile food vendor fire inspections
Recommend address corrections to Board of Trustees for E911 compliance
Recommend Board authorize raze order for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property concern at Sternberg and South Timber
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 6th at 5:00 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.

Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
